subject: Mathematics

lesson_overview.subject: Mathematics
lesson_overview.curriculum_reference: Recognise, find and write fractions of a discrete set of objects: unit fractions and non-unit fractions with small denominators.

lesson_rationale: This lesson introduces pupils to the idea of one half in a way that matches the thinking style of younger children. The lesson begins with a familiar sharing situation so pupils can connect the maths idea to real life. It then uses clear visual models, simple language, talk-based questioning, hands-on folding, colouring, and a short game. Because pupils at this age often notice that something has been split into two parts without noticing whether the parts are equal, the lesson repeatedly reinforces the idea of equal parts. The overall structure follows a see it, say it, do it, and explain it pattern so that pupils can build confidence step by step.

age_group_characteristics: Pupils learn best from concrete examples before abstract symbols.
Pupils benefit from repetition and short activity changes.
Pupils need simple spoken language and clear visual support.
Pupils often understand fairness before formal fraction notation.
Pupils may struggle to explain abstract reasoning without sentence support.
design_response: The lesson starts with sharing food fairly.
The teacher uses pictures, folding, and shading before formal notation.
Key ideas are repeated several times in different forms.
Activities alternate between listening, talking, doing, and checking.
Teacher questions use simple prompts such as 'How do you know?' and 'Are they the same size?'

knowledge: Pupils are familiar with sharing objects between two people.
Pupils can recognise simple 2D shapes such as circles, squares, and rectangles.
Pupils understand everyday words such as whole, piece, and same.
skills: Pupils can count small groups of objects.
Pupils can colour or shade simple shapes.
Pupils can talk to a partner and answer simple class questions.
prior_learning.connections_to_previous_learning: This lesson builds on pupils' early understanding of fair sharing and prepares them for later learning about quarters, thirds, and finding fractions of quantities.

learning_objectives: Recognise one half as one of two equal parts of a whole.
Spot when a shape or object has been split into halves correctly.
Use simple maths words such as half, whole, equal, part, and fraction.
Show one half by folding, shading, or drawing.
success_criteria: I can say that one half means one of two equal parts.
I can tell if two parts are the same size.
I can colour one half of a shape correctly.
I can say why something is or is not one half.

key_vocabulary.definition: A number that shows part of a whole.

key_vocabulary.child_example: Half a pizza means one of two equal pieces.

key_vocabulary.definition: The complete thing before it is split.
key_vocabulary.child_example: The full cake is one whole cake.
Both pieces are equal because they are the same size.
One slice is one part of the pizza.

recommended_phrases: Are the parts the same size?
This is not one half because...
sentence_stems_for_pupils: I think this is one half because...
I know it is not one half because...
The two parts are not the same size.

resources_summary: Pizza or sandwich picture for starter
Prepared slides with correct and incorrect examples of half
Paper circles and rectangles for folding activity
Printed worksheet with colouring and matching tasks

external_resources.title: BBC Bitesize – Fractions
external_resources.description: Simple interactive explanations and activities for children learning fractions.

external_resources.title: White Rose Education – Fractions Resources
external_resources.description: Structured primary maths resources aligned with UK classroom teaching.
external_resources.suggested_use: Teacher preparation and main activity support

external_resources.title: NRICH
external_resources.description: Reasoning-rich maths activities suitable for extension and challenge.

external_resources.title: Twinkl
external_resources.description: Printable teaching materials including fraction worksheets and visuals.

anticipated_misconceptions.issue: Pupils may think any shape split into two parts shows one half.
anticipated_misconceptions.response: The teacher repeatedly checks whether the two parts are the same size.

anticipated_misconceptions.issue: Pupils may think one half only works when a shape is split vertically.
anticipated_misconceptions.response: The teacher shows horizontal, diagonal, and other equal splits.

anticipated_misconceptions.issue: Pupils may focus on one shaded part and ignore the total number of parts.
anticipated_misconceptions.response: The teacher asks both 'How many parts are there?' and 'Are they equal?'

anticipated_misconceptions.issue: Pupils may use informal language without understanding the fraction meaning.
anticipated_misconceptions.response: The teacher models and repeats simple sentence stems using maths vocabulary.

interactive_activity.name: Find the Half
interactive_activity.description: The teacher shows pictures quickly on the board. Pupils point, hold up a card, or use thumbs up/down to show whether the picture shows one half.
interactive_activity.purpose: To give repeated, fast-paced practice in spotting correct and incorrect examples.

teaching_sequence.purpose: To connect the lesson to a familiar real-life idea of fair sharing.
teacher_actions: Show a large picture of one pizza or sandwich on the board.
Say, 'Two children want to share this fairly. What should happen?'
Give pupils 20 to 30 seconds to talk to a partner.
Take a few answers from the class.
Repeat the key idea that fair means the same size.
Introduce the word half.
student_activities: Look at the picture.
Talk to a partner.
Say that each person should get the same amount.
Begin to use the word half.
teacher_questions: How can we share this fairly?
Do both people need the same amount?
Would it still be fair if one piece was bigger?
expected_pupil_responses: Both people should get the same amount.
No, it is not fair if one piece is bigger.
assessment_opportunities: Check whether pupils connect half with equal sharing.
Listen for children who say 'two pieces' but do not mention same size.

teaching_sequence.purpose: To teach the meaning of one half using simple visual models and child-friendly explanation.
teacher_actions: Show one whole circle on the board and say, 'This is one whole.'
Split it into two equal parts and shade one part.
Write 1/2 and read it aloud as 'one half'.
Say, 'One half means one of two equal parts.'
Point to each part and compare sizes.
Then show a wrong example where one side is bigger and ask if it is half.
Repeat the phrase 'equal parts' several times.
student_activities: Look at the whole shape.
Watch it split into two equal parts.
Say 'one half' aloud.
Answer whether examples are right or wrong.
teaching_sequence.teacher_explanation: A whole can be split into parts. If the whole is split into two equal parts, one of those parts is called one half. The parts must be the same size. If one part is bigger, it is not one half.
teacher_questions: Why is this not one half?
expected_pupil_responses: Yes, they are the same size.
It is one half because it is one of two equal parts.
It is not one half because one side is bigger.
assessment_opportunities: Use thumbs up/down after each example.
Ask individual pupils to finish the sentence stem.

teaching_sequence.purpose: To reinforce understanding through a fast, playful game.
teacher_actions: Play the game 'Find the Half'.
Show several pictures one by one on the board.
Ask pupils to show thumbs up if it is one half and thumbs down if it is not.
Pause after each slide and ask one or two pupils to explain.
student_activities: Look carefully at each picture.
Respond quickly with thumbs up/down or cards.
Say why an example is right or wrong.
expected_pupil_responses: Yes, because the two parts are equal.
No, because the shape has three parts.
No, because one part is bigger.
assessment_opportunities: Quickly identify children who are still unsure.
Use explanations to check understanding beyond guessing.

teaching_sequence.purpose: To support pupils in recognising one half with teacher guidance before independent work.
teacher_actions: Show several shapes on the board.
Model how to check: count the parts, compare the size, then decide.
Complete one example together as a class.
Ask pupils to talk to a partner before answering.
student_activities: Join in with teacher-led checking.
Say whether a picture shows one half.
Use sentence stems with a partner.
teacher_questions: First, how many parts are there?
So, does it show one half?
support_strategies: Point to each section as pupils count.
Give sentence stems on the board.
assessment_opportunities: Listen for correct use of the checking method.
Notice who still needs concrete support.

teaching_sequence.purpose: To let pupils physically and visually represent one half.
teacher_actions: Give out paper circles, rectangles, and worksheets.
Model folding one paper shape into two equal parts.
Show how to colour one half carefully.
Explain worksheet tasks one at a time.
Move around the classroom and provide immediate feedback.
student_activities: Fold shapes into two equal parts.
Open the shapes and colour one half.
Complete worksheet questions.
Circle correct examples and cross incorrect ones.
independent_tasks: Task 1: Fold a paper circle into two equal parts and colour one half.
Task 2: Shade one half of simple printed shapes.
Task 3: Circle pictures that show one half correctly.
Task 4: Cross out pictures that do not show one half.
support_adaptations: Reduce the number of worksheet items.
Check each response with the pupil immediately.
challenge_extension: Draw your own shape and show one half in a different way.
Explain whether a triangle can show one half.
expected_outcomes: Most pupils correctly colour one half of a familiar shape.
Most pupils identify when parts are not equal.
Some pupils apply the idea to sets of objects.
assessment_opportunities: Ask pupils to explain one answer verbally.

teaching_sequence.purpose: To review learning and strengthen memory through simple repetition and explanation.
teacher_actions: Bring the class back together.
Ask pupils to repeat the key sentence: 'One half means one of two equal parts.'
Invite pupils to give examples from everyday life.
Review one correct and one incorrect example quickly.
Praise accurate vocabulary and clear explanations.
student_activities: Say the key sentence together.
Give examples such as half a sandwich or half a pizza.
Answer simple recap questions.
teacher_questions: What word tells us the parts must be the same size?
Can you tell me one real-life example of half?
expected_pupil_responses: One half means one of two equal parts.
Half a sandwich, half a pizza, half a cake.
assessment_opportunities: Identify pupils who may need more practice next lesson.

support.description: Pupils who need extra support will work with concrete materials, such as folded paper shapes, and may complete fewer questions. The teacher or teaching assistant will use short prompts and repeated questions.

core.description: Most pupils will complete the full set of shape-based tasks and explain whether examples show one half using the taught vocabulary.

challenge.description: Higher-attaining pupils will apply the idea of one half to sets of objects and explain why half can look different in different shapes.

assessment_for_learning.informal_questioning: Simple oral questions are used throughout the lesson to check whether pupils understand equal parts.
assessment_for_learning.live_visual_checks: Thumbs up/down and mini whiteboards help the teacher see who understands quickly.
assessment_for_learning.observation: The teacher watches how pupils fold, shade, and discuss examples during guided and independent work.
assessment_for_learning.worksheet_evidence: Completed colouring and identification tasks show whether pupils can apply the idea independently.
assessment_for_learning.spoken_reasoning: Pupils explain in simple language why something is or is not one half.

homework.main_task: Draw three everyday objects and show one half of each.
homework.written_reflection: Finish the sentence: One half means...
homework.extension: Find one example of half at home and tell the class next lesson.

teacher_reflection_prompts: Did pupils understand that the two parts must be equal, not just two pieces?
Which visual examples worked best for this age group?
Did the game improve confidence and participation?
Were the sentence stems enough to support spoken reasoning?
Which pupils are ready for learning about quarters next?
